stem: The series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n^{[[a]]}\,\sqrt[[[b]]]{n}} \) is a \( p \)-series of the form \( \sum 1/n^{p} \). What is the value of \( p \)?
distractor_rationales: This ignores the root factor, using only the integer power of \( n \).
This adds the root index \( [[b]] \) instead of its reciprocal \( 1/[[b]] \).
This subtracts the root's exponent instead of adding it.
This multiplies the exponents rather than adding them.
This treats the root as contributing \( 1 \) to the exponent.
This keeps only the root's exponent and drops \( n^{[[a]]} \).

stem: The series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n^{[[a]]}\cdot n^{[[b]]}} \) is a \( p \)-series of the form \( \sum 1/n^{p} \). What is the value of \( p \)?
solution: Combine the powers of \( n \): \( n^{[[a]]}\cdot n^{[[b]]} = n^{[[a]]+[[b]]} \), so \( p = [[a]]+[[b]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the exponents instead of adding them.
This keeps only the first factor's exponent.
This keeps only the second factor's exponent.
This subtracts the exponents instead of adding them.
This is off by one from the correct exponent.
This is off by one from the correct exponent.

stem: The series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{\sqrt[[[b]]]{n^{[[a]]}}} \) is a \( p \)-series of the form \( \sum 1/n^{p} \). What is the value of \( p \)?
solution: Write the root as an exponent: \( \sqrt[[[b]]]{n^{[[a]]}} = n^{[[a]]/[[b]]} \), so \( p = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the root entirely, using only the inner power \( a \).
This ignores the inner power entirely, using only the root index \( b \).
This inverts the exponent \( a/b \).
This multiplies \( a \) and \( b \) instead of dividing.
This is off by one from the correct exponent.
This is off by one from the correct exponent.

stem: The series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{\sqrt[[[c]]]{n}}{n^{[[a]]}} \) is a \( p \)-series of the form \( \sum 1/n^{p} \). What is the value of \( p \)?
solution: Write the numerator's root as an exponent: \( \sqrt[[[c]]]{n} = n^{1/[[c]]} \), so the whole term is \( n^{1/[[c]] - [[a]]} \), i.e. \( \dfrac{1}{n^{[[a]] - 1/[[c]]}} \). Hence \( p = [[a]] - \dfrac{1}{[[c]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the numerator's root factor entirely.
This adds the root's exponent instead of subtracting it (the root is in the numerator, reducing \( p \)).
This has the wrong sign on the whole expression.
This multiplies the exponents rather than combining them by subtraction.
This is off by one from the correct exponent.
This is off by one from the correct exponent.
